# Study Notes: Plant Pathology (3201) - Section A (Sabiha Mam) Welcome to your comprehensive study notes. Navigate directly to any lecture below: ### 📂 Table of Contents - [Lecture 1.0 Introduction and History of Plant Pathology](#1-0-introduction-and-history-of-plant-pathology) - [Lecture 1.1 Pathogenesis & Infection Process](#1-1-pathogenesis-infection-process) - [Lecture 1.2 Toxin and Host Defense](#1-2-toxin-and-host-defense) - [Lecture 1.3 Effects of Pathogens on Plant Physiological Functions](#1-3-effects-of-pathogens-on-plant-physiological-functions) - [Lecture 2.1 Dissemination of Pathogens](#2-1-dissemination-of-pathogens) - [Lecture 2.2 Epidemiology of Plant Diseases](#2-2-epidemiology-of-plant-diseases) - [Lecture 2.2 Epidemiology - Core/Important Concepts](#2-2-epidemiology-core-important-concepts) - [Lecture 3.1 Methods of Plant Disease Control](#3-1-methods-of-plant-disease-control) - [Lecture 3.2 Biological Control of Plant Diseases](#3-2-biological-control-of-plant-diseases) - [Lecture 3.3 Chemical Control & Fungicides](#3-3-chemical-control-fungicides) ---
# Lecture: 1.0 Introduction and History of Plant Pathology # Course Slides: 1.0 Introduction and History of Plant Pathology ## Slide 1 ![Diagram: Slide 1 Image 1](/uploads/1.0 Introduction and History of Plant Pathology_s1_img1.jpg) --- ## Slide 2 What is plant pathology ? Plant Pathology (Phytopathology): Greek Word Three words: Phyton + Pathos + logos Plant + suffering/ailments + study/knowledge Definition: Plant Pathology is that branch of agricultural, botanical or biological science. --- ## Slide 3 What is plant pathology ? Plant pathology deals- Study of the causes, etiology, resulting losses and management/ control of plant diseases. Study of the organisms and environmental factors that cause diseases in plants, mechanisms by which they cause disease, and methods of managing diseases and reducing damage. --- ## Slide 4 Why do we study Plant Pathology? To study the biotic, abiotic and environmental causes of plant diseases To study the interaction(s) between the plant and the pathogen To study the mechanism(s) of disease development. To develop management strategy of the diseases --- ## Slide 5 What is the importance of Plant Pathology in Agriculture ? Plant pathology can and should contribute in each of these respects— by assessing the immediate and potential dangers to crops from diseases by forecasting their incidence and severity by deploying the best methods of control in the short and longer term by evaluating the risks particularly by integrated use of the management practices. --- ## Slide 6 ![Diagram: Slide 6 Image 1](/uploads/1.0 Introduction and History of Plant Pathology_s6_img1.png) ![Diagram: Slide 6 Image 2](/uploads/1.0 Introduction and History of Plant Pathology_s6_img2.jpg) History of Plant Pathology --- ## Slide 7 History of Plant Pathology Four Phases Ancient period Dark period Renaissance period Modern period --- ## Slide 8 Ancient Period Theophrastus (300-286 B.C.) Study and write about the diseases (rust, mildews, blight)of trees, cereals and legumes in his book enquiry into plants. His approach was observational and speculative rather than experimental. Theory of spontaneous generation 2 Books : Historia Plantarum De Causis Plantarum ![Diagram: Slide 8 Image 1](/uploads/1.0 Introduction and History of Plant Pathology_s8_img1.jpg) Father of botany --- ## Slide 9 Albertus Magnus (1200 A.D.) ![Diagram: Slide 9 Image 1](/uploads/1.0 Introduction and History of Plant Pathology_s9_img1.jpg) Recognized Mistletoe as the first plant pathogen and the first pathogen for which a cultural control (by pruning affected branches) was recommended. --- ## Slide 10 DARK PERIOD 300AD and 1300AD Also called pre-renaissance period in history No increase in the knowledge of plant pathology An Arabian Ibnal Awan described symptoms & control measures of many trees & vine diseases. --- ## Slide 11 PRE-MODERN/ RENAISSANCE PERIOD/AUTOGENIC ERA (17th –mid 19th) ![Diagram: Slide 11 Image 1](/uploads/1.0 Introduction and History of Plant Pathology_s11_img1.jpg) 1675 A. D.-Anton van Leeuwenhoek (Holland) Invented compound microscope in 1675 Examined anatomy of plants and body of filamentous fungus, algae, protozoa, sperm cells, blood cells and bacteria. In 1683 he described bacteria seen with this microscope Father of Microbiology --- ## Slide 12 1729 A. D.-Pier Antonio Micheli (Italian) Observed fungal spores for the first time and conducted studies related to many spores germination in 1729 He published a book “Nova Plantarum Genera” in which he gave descriptions about 1900 species in Latin out of which 900 were fungi. ![Diagram: Slide 12 Image 1](/uploads/1.0 Introduction and History of Plant Pathology_s12_img1.jpg) ![Diagram: Slide 12 Image 2](/uploads/1.0 Introduction and History of Plant Pathology_s12_img2.jpg) Father of Mycology --- ## Slide 13 1755 A. D.-Tillet (France) Published a paper on bunt or stinking smut of wheat He reported the chemical treatment of seeds --- ## Slide 14 MODERN PERIOD/ GOLDEN ERA/PATHOGENIC ERA (1800 –--)) ![Diagram: Slide 14 Image 1](/uploads/1.0 Introduction and History of Plant Pathology_s14_img1.jpg) IRIS FAMINE In 1845, the potato crop in Ireland was completely wiped out by late blight disease Caused great famine in 1846. This resulted in the death of hundreds of thousands of people Immigration of more than one and a half million Irish to United States. ![Diagram: Slide 14 Image 2](/uploads/1.0 Introduction and History of Plant Pathology_s14_img2.png) Irish famine Monument --- ## Slide 15 ![Diagram: Slide 15 Image 1](/uploads/1.0 Introduction and History of Plant Pathology_s15_img1.jpg) Anton De Bary (1853 A. D).Germany) Established that fungi are the causes, not result of plant diseases Renamed the casual organism of late blight as Phytophthora infestans. He discovered heteroecious nature of rust fungi Detailed life cycles of downy mildew He wrote a book named “Morphology and Physiology of fungi, lichens and Myxomycetes” (1866). Father of Modern Mycology --- ## Slide 16 ![Diagram: Slide 16 Image 1](/uploads/1.0 Introduction and History of Plant Pathology_s16_img1.png) Louis Pasteur Established the germ theory In 1864, Pasteur invented pasteurization in 1880, made the first vaccine against the chicken cholera. Germ theory Microorganisms were produced from preexisting microorganisms Most infectious diseases were caused by germs. --- ## Slide 17 ![Diagram: Slide 17 Image 1](/uploads/1.0 Introduction and History of Plant Pathology_s17_img1.png) Robert Koch (1879 A. D.) Established Koch’s postulates(rules of disease diagnosis) In 1876 identified the anthrax bacillus, Bacillus anthracis, as the first bacterium to cause disease in animals and humans. Father of Bacteriology ![Diagram: Slide 17 Image 2](/uploads/1.0 Introduction and History of Plant Pathology_s17_img2.jpg) --- ## Slide 18 ![Diagram: Slide 18 Image 1](/uploads/1.0 Introduction and History of Plant Pathology_s18_img1.png) ![Diagram: Slide 18 Image 2](/uploads/1.0 Introduction and History of Plant Pathology_s18_img2.jpg) Bacillus anthracis ![Diagram: Slide 18 Image 3](/uploads/1.0 Introduction and History of Plant Pathology_s18_img3.jpg) --- ## Slide 19 ![Diagram: Slide 19 Image 1](/uploads/1.0 Introduction and History of Plant Pathology_s19_img1.jpg) --- ## Slide 20 ![Diagram: Slide 20 Image 1](/uploads/1.0 Introduction and History of Plant Pathology_s20_img1.png) 1889 A. D. Beijerinck (Dutch) proved that the virus is not a living microorganism. N.A. Cobb Contributed a lot to nematode morphology, taxonomy and methodology Founder of Virology ![Diagram: Slide 20 Image 2](/uploads/1.0 Introduction and History of Plant Pathology_s20_img2.jpg) Founder of Nematology --- ## Slide 21 ![Diagram: Slide 21 Image 1](/uploads/1.0 Introduction and History of Plant Pathology_s21_img1.jpg) Paul Neergaard He helped to standardize methods for the detection of seed borne fungi. "Seed Pathology" (1977) which served as a reference and teaching standard for the science of seed pathology throughout the world. Father of seed pathology --- ## Slide 22 Plant Pathology Bangladesh Perspective --- ## Slide 23 Bengal Famine 1943 (more 3-4 million people died) One of the causes of famine was Brown spot disease of rice ![Diagram: Slide 23 Image 1](/uploads/1.0 Introduction and History of Plant Pathology_s23_img1.jpg) ![Diagram: Slide 23 Image 2](/uploads/1.0 Introduction and History of Plant Pathology_s23_img2.png) --- ## Slide 24 Bangladesh perspective Department of plant pathology Seed Pathology Centre Plant Disease diagnostic Clinic --- ## Slide 25 ![Diagram: Slide 25 Image 1](/uploads/1.0 Introduction and History of Plant Pathology_s25_img1.jpg) --- ---
# Lecture: 1.1 Pathogenesis & Infection Process # Course Slides: 1.1 Pathogenesis ## Slide 1 Chapter 1 Pathogenesis ![Diagram: Slide 1 Image 1](/uploads/1.1 Pathogenesis_s1_img1.jpg) --- ## Slide 2 Intended Learning Outcomes Differentiate parasitism and pathogenicity State sequential events in disease development ![Diagram: Slide 2 Image 1](/uploads/1.1 Pathogenesis_s2_img1.jpg) --- ## Slide 3 Q Parasite: An organism that lives on and derives food from another organism. Parasitism: The relationship between a parasite and its host is parasitism. Pathogen: An agent that can incite a disease. Pathogenicity: It is the ability of the pathogen to cause disease. ![Diagram: Slide 3 Image 1](/uploads/1.1 Pathogenesis_s3_img1.jpg) --- ## Slide 4 Parasitism and Pathogenicity Most successful pathogens fungi, bacteria, mollicutes, parasitic higher plants, nematodes, protozoa, viruses and viroids --- ## Slide 5 Q Where the viruses, viroids, mycoplasmas, and fastidious bacteria overseason? ![Diagram: Slide 5 Image 1](/uploads/1.1 Pathogenesis_s5_img1.png) --- ## Slide 6 Parasitism and Pathogenicity Are all pathogens parasites? A parasite is not necessarily a pathogen and a pathogen is not necessarily a parasite But why? --- ## Slide 7 Parasitism and Pathogenicity Symbiosis such as mycorrhizae or root nodule bacteria. ![Diagram: Slide 7 Image 1](/uploads/1.1 Pathogenesis_s7_img1.jpg) --- ## Slide 8 Symbiotic Relationship is a type of parasitism, explain. Q Symbiotic relationship refers to any close and long-term interaction between two organisms, including mutualism, commensalism, and parasitism. Parasitism is a specific type of symbiosis where one organism, the parasite, benefits by exploiting the host for nutrients, water, or shelter, causing harm to the host. Pathogen like fungi (Puccinia graminis) is a plant parasite. These pathogens attach to, penetrate, infect, and invade host tissues, leading to symptoms such as necrosis, wilting, and reduced yield. While all parasitism is symbiotic, not all symbiosis is parasitic, as mutualism and commensalism do not harm the host. --- ## Slide 9 Parasitism and Pathogenicity Biotrophs/Obligate Parasites Non-obligate (2) Facultative parasite/Necrotrophs. Facultative saprophytes/Hemibiotrophs /semibiotrophs --- ## Slide 10 Q ![Diagram: Slide 10 Image 1](/uploads/1.1 Pathogenesis_s10_img1.jpg) Obligate Parasites --- ## Slide 11 ![Diagram: Slide 11 Image 1](/uploads/1.1 Pathogenesis_s11_img1.jpg) Q --- ## Slide 12 Characteristics of Necrotrophs Biochemical and morphological features: Host cell rapidly killed Toxins and cytolytic enzyme produced No special parasitic structures formed Host penetration via wounds or natural openings --- ## Slide 13 Characteristics of Necrotrophs Ecological features Wide host range Able to grow saprophytically away from the host Attack juvenile, debilitated or senescing tissues --- ## Slide 14 Characteristics of Biotrophs Biochemical and morphological features: Host cells not rapidly killed Few or no toxins or cytolytic enzymes produced Special parasitic structures eg, haustoria are formed Host penetration direct or via natural openings --- ## Slide 15 Characteristics of Biotrophs Ecological features Narrow host range Unable to grow away from the host Attack healthy hosts at all stages of development --- ## Slide 16 Pathogenesis/Disease cycle The chain of events involved in disease development is called a disease cycle or pathogenesis ![Diagram: Slide 16 Image 1](/uploads/1.1 Pathogenesis_s16_img1.png) Q --- ## Slide 17 ![Diagram: Slide 17 Image 1](/uploads/1.1 Pathogenesis_s17_img1.png) Q --- ## Slide 18 Q Stages in the development of disease Inoculation Pre-penetration Phenomena Penetration Infection Invasion Growth and Reproduction of the Pathogen Dissemination of the Pathogen Over-wintering/over-summering of the Pathogen --- ## Slide 19 Q Pathogenesis: The chain of events involved in disease development, including the stages of development of the pathogen and the effect of disease on the host is called disease cycle or pathogenesis. What Happens If Colonization Doesn’t Occur In Disease Cyle ?? Disruption of disease cycle No establishment of pathogen Colonization: In Pathogenesis, It refers to the successful establishment and multiplication of the pathogen within the host tissue after the infection has occurred. Limited pathogen spread No infection or disease development ![Diagram: Slide 19 Image 1](/uploads/1.1 Pathogenesis_s19_img1.png) ![Diagram: Slide 19 Image 2](/uploads/1.1 Pathogenesis_s19_img2.jpg) Pathogen survival is jeopardized Without colonization and the subsequent growth and reproduction of the pathogen, the disease cycle is effectively interrupted resulting the host remains unaffected, the pathogen fails to thrive, and transmission to other hosts does not occur, ultimately leading to the pathogen's diminished survival and spread. --- ## Slide 20 Q ![Diagram: Slide 20 Image 1](/uploads/1.1 Pathogenesis_s20_img1.png) ![Diagram: Slide 20 Image 2](/uploads/1.1 Pathogenesis_s20_img2.png) --- ## Slide 21 Q ![Diagram: Slide 21 Image 1](/uploads/1.1 Pathogenesis_s21_img1.png) --- ## Slide 22 Q Pathogen: An entity that can incite disease. Inoculum: The pathogen or its parts that can cause infection; that portion of individual pathogens that are brought into contact with the host. --- ## Slide 23 Inoculation Inoculation Inoculum Propagule --- ## Slide 24 Which are Inocula? ![Diagram: Slide 24 Image 1](/uploads/1.1 Pathogenesis_s24_img1.jpg) Fungus Bacteria, mollicutes, protozoa, viruses and viroids. ![Diagram: Slide 24 Image 2](/uploads/1.1 Pathogenesis_s24_img2.jpg) ![Diagram: Slide 24 Image 3](/uploads/1.1 Pathogenesis_s24_img3.gif) ![Diagram: Slide 24 Image 4](/uploads/1.1 Pathogenesis_s24_img4.jpg) --- ## Slide 25 Which are Inocula? Nematodes Phanerogamic parasites ![Diagram: Slide 25 Image 1](/uploads/1.1 Pathogenesis_s25_img1.jpg) ![Diagram: Slide 25 Image 2](/uploads/1.1 Pathogenesis_s25_img2.jpg) --- ## Slide 26 Q Types of Inoculum Primary inoculum and primary infection (Carries itself to the host body by itself) Secondary inoculum and secondary infection (Produced in the affected plant) --- ## Slide 27 Sources of inoculum Inside sources Outside sources --- ## Slide 28 Arrival or landing of inoculum on host Most is carried by wind, water, and insects. Only a small number actually lands on susceptible plants. May be carried by vectors. --- ## Slide 29 Pre-penetration Phenomena --- ## Slide 30 Q i) Attachment of pathogen to host The propagules of pathogen secrete mucilaginous substances which helps to adhere to the plant. ![Diagram: Slide 30 Image 1](/uploads/1.1 Pathogenesis_s30_img1.jpg) ii) Spore germination and perception of the host surface iii) Appressorium Formation and Maturation ![Diagram: Slide 30 Image 2](/uploads/1.1 Pathogenesis_s30_img2.jpg) iv) Recognition between Host and Pathogen The components released from both of host and pathogen are responsible for the recognition. --- ## Slide 31 Attachment of Pathogens to Host Viruses, mollicutes, fastidious bacteria, and protozoa. Almost all fungi, bacteria and parasitic plants. --- ## Slide 32 Attachment of Pathogens to Host The propagules have a mucilaginous layer. The germ tubes also produce these substances. The areas of contact appear to degrade due to enzymes. --- ## Slide 33 Germination of spores and seeds Requirements for germination Favorable temperature Favorable moisture in the form of rain, dew, or a film of water --- ## Slide 34 Pre-penetration Phenomena Germination of spores and seeds Spore germination is often favored by Exudates Certain pathogens are only stimulated by exudates of plants susceptible to that particular pathogen. --- ## Slide 35 Pre-penetration Phenomena Germination of spores and seeds Spore germination may be inhibited by materials released by the plant by saprophytic microflora --- ## Slide 36 Pre-penetration Phenomena Pre-penetration Phenomena Germination of spores and seeds Antagonistic microorganisms Fungistasis Suppressive soils --- ## Slide 37 Prepenetration Phenomena Pre-penetration Phenomena ![Diagram: Slide 37 Image 1](/uploads/1.1 Pathogenesis_s37_img1.png) ![Diagram: Slide 37 Image 2](/uploads/1.1 Pathogenesis_s37_img2.png) A rust uredospore that has germinated and produced a dome-like appressorium. Uredospores of a rust fungus next to open stomata. --- ## Slide 38 Pre-penetration Phenomena Pre-penetration Phenomena After spores germinate the resulting germ tubes must grow or the secondary zoospores must move toward a penetration site The number, length and rate of growth of germ tubes or the number and mobility of motile spores affected by temperature and moisture etc. --- ## Slide 39 Prepenetration Phenomena Pre-penetration Phenomena Chemical stimuli associated with wounds, stomata and lenticels also stimulate growth Seeds germinate by producing a radicle --- ## Slide 40 Hatching of Nematode Eggs Favorable temperature and moisture The egg contains the first juvenile stage before or soon after the egg is laid. This juvenile immediately undergoes a molt and gives rise to the second juvenile stage, which may remain dormant in the egg for various periods of time. --- ## Slide 41 Hatching of Nematode Eggs Thus, when the egg finally hatches, it is the second-stage juvenile that emerges, and it either finds and penetrates a host plant or undergoes additional molts that produce further juvenile stages and adults. --- ## Slide 42 Hatching of Nematode Eggs They are attracted to the root by CO2 and some amino acids associated with root growth --- ## Slide 43 Q Penetration Pathogens penetrate plant surfaces through natural openings or wounds or directly Some fungi penetrate only in one way, others in several ways Bacteria enter mostly through wounds sometimes through natural openings but never directly Viruses, viroids, mollicutes fastidious bacteria and protozoa enter through wounds by vectors --- ## Slide 44 ![Diagram: Slide 44 Image 1](/uploads/1.1 Pathogenesis_s44_img1.png) Q --- ## Slide 45 Q ![Diagram: Slide 45 Image 1](/uploads/1.1 Pathogenesis_s45_img1.jpg) --- ## Slide 46 Q Direct penetration through intact plant surfaces/ Use of physical weapons Fungi, nematodes and parasitic plants. Fungi use fine hyphae. ![Diagram: Slide 46 Image 1](/uploads/1.1 Pathogenesis_s46_img1.jpg) --- ## Slide 47 Q Direct penetration through intact plant surfaces The penetration is through mechanical force and softening of the cell walls by an enzyme. Most fungi form an appressorium at the end of the germ tube. Then a penetration peg emerges from the flat surface of the appressorium and pierces the cuticle and cell wall. --- ## Slide 48 Q Direct penetration through intact plant surfaces The peg grows into fine hyphae then reaches a normal diameter once it is inside the cell Parasitic plants also penetrate the same way --- ## Slide 49 Q Direct penetration through intact plant surfaces Nematodes penetrate its stylet Inserts its stylet or the entire nematode enters ![Diagram: Slide 49 Image 1](/uploads/1.1 Pathogenesis_s49_img1.png) --- ## Slide 50 Penetration through wounds Bacteria, most fungi, some viruses, all viroids Viruses mollicutes, fastidious vascular bacteria, and protozoa enter through wounds made by vectors Some pathogens can be only carried by specific vectors --- ## Slide 51 Q Penetration through natural openings Many fungi and bacteria enter through: Stomata Hydathodes Nectarthodes Lenticels --- ## Slide 52 Q ![Diagram: Slide 52 Image 1](/uploads/1.1 Pathogenesis_s52_img1.jpg) --- ## Slide 53 Penetration through stomata Underside of the leaf Bacteria swim Fungi can germinate on a wet surface and enter a stomata --- ## Slide 54 Hydathodes Open pores on the margins and tips of leaves Connected to veins Secrete droplets of liquids containing nutrients some bacteria but few fungi enter here Some enter through nectarthodes which are similar to hydathodes but on blossoms --- ## Slide 55 Lenticels Openings on fruits, stems, and tubers, filled with loosely connected cells to allow passage of air Relatively few fungi and bacterial enter this way A less efficient, secondary pathway --- ## Slide 56 Path of Entry Fungi = direct, wounds, natural openings Bacteria = wounds, natural openings Nematodes = direct, natural openings Parasitic higher plants = direct Virus, viroids, mollicutes, protozoa and fastidious bacteria =wounds --- ## Slide 57 Q Infection Establishment of contact by the pathogens with the host plant is called infection. During infection, the pathogen grows or multiplies, colonizes the host plant Infection results in the appearance of symptoms Some infections remain latent and show up when conditions are more favorable --- ## Slide 58 Infection Symptoms may show up in 2-4 days or as long as 2-3 years Incubation period In most plants the incubation period is from a few days to a few weeks --- ## Slide 59 Infection During infection some pathogens: Obtain nutrients without killing the cell Kill cells and use contents Kill cells and disorganize surrounding tissue Release enzymes, toxins, growth regulators --- ## Slide 60 Q Successful infection may or may not always result in appearance of symptoms, explain with examples: A successful infection occurs when a pathogen invades a host plant, establishes itself, and begins to multiply. However, this does not always lead to visible symptoms of disease. Scenarios Where Successful Infection May Not Cause Symptoms Latent Infections: Pathogens infect the host and remain dormant or inactive for some time before symptoms appear. Example: Apple’s latent virus. --- ## Slide 61 Q 2. Tolerant Host Plants: Some plants are infected but can resist or tolerate the pathogen's presence without showing symptoms. Example: Some wheat varieties can host Barley yellow dwarf virus. 3. Environmental Conditions: The environment may not be conducive to disease development even if infection occurs. Example: Phytophthora infestans (late blight pathogen) may infect potatoes, but symptoms won’t develop in dry conditions. --- ## Slide 62 Q Invasion The spread of the pathogen into the host is called invasion. Apple scab grows between the cuticle and the epidermis ![Diagram: Slide 62 Image 1](/uploads/1.1 Pathogenesis_s62_img1.png) --- ## Slide 63 Invasion ![Diagram: Slide 63 Image 1](/uploads/1.1 Pathogenesis_s63_img1.png) Powdery mildew grow on the surface and send haustoria into epidermal cells --- ## Slide 64 ![Diagram: Slide 64 Image 1](/uploads/1.1 Pathogenesis_s64_img1.png) ![Diagram: Slide 64 Image 2](/uploads/1.1 Pathogenesis_s64_img2.png) intercellularly ![Diagram: Slide 64 Image 3](/uploads/1.1 Pathogenesis_s64_img3.png) Hyphae of the smut fungus Ustilago in an infected leaf (intracellularly) vascular wilts invade the xylem vessels (Both fungus and bacteria) --- ## Slide 65 Invasion Fungi invade intercellularly and intracellularly and some grow into and throughout the plant Bacteria at first invade intercellularly and then intracellularly. Bacteria causing vascular wilts, invade the xylem vessels --- ## Slide 66 ![Diagram: Slide 66 Image 1](/uploads/1.1 Pathogenesis_s66_img1.png) ![Diagram: Slide 66 Image 2](/uploads/1.1 Pathogenesis_s66_img2.png) Alfalfa shoot invaded by plant parasitic nematodes (Ditylenchus dipsaci) --- ## Slide 67 Invasion Most nematodes are intercellular some intracellular and some don’t invade at all but feed by piercing epidermal cells with their stylets. Viruses, viroids, mollicutes, fastidious bacteria, and protozoa invade by moving from cell to cell --- ## Slide 68 Types of cells and tissues invaded Fungi, viruses, and viroids invade all types of cells Mollicutes and protozoa invade phloem sieve tubes and some parenchyma Most fastidious bacteria invade xylem vessels and a few invade phloem sieve tubes --- ## Slide 69 Local and Systemic invasion Fungi, bacteria, nematodes, viruses, and parasitic plants : local Fastidious bacteria, mollicutes, and protozoa: systemic (internal) Some fungi, bacteria, and viruses are also systemic --- ## Slide 70 Difference between infection and Invasion ![Diagram: Slide 70 Image 1](/uploads/1.1 Pathogenesis_s70_img1.png) --- ## Slide 71 Growth and Reproduction of the Pathogen Fungi= spores, sexual or asexual Parasitic plants= seeds on branches Bacteria, mollicutes, protozoa = fission Viruses and viroids= replicate Nematodes = eggs --- ## Slide 72 Growth and Reproduction of the Pathogen Location of reproduction Most fungi reproduce inside the plant but most release spores outside Powdery mildew on the outside Viruses, viroids, mollicutes, protozoa, and fastidious bacteria only reproduce inside --- ## Slide 73 ![Diagram: Slide 73 Image 1](/uploads/1.1 Pathogenesis_s73_img1.png) ![Diagram: Slide 73 Image 2](/uploads/1.1 Pathogenesis_s73_img2.png) --- ## Slide 74 Growth and Reproduction of the Pathogen Rate of reproduction Fungi: millions of spores in a season Bacteria divide every 20-30 minutes Viruses can produce 100,000 to 10 million particles in a single cell Nematodes lay 300-500 eggs about half female who do the same and so on 2 to 8 million, nematodes are reproduced in one season during one season --- ## Slide 75 ![Diagram: Slide 75 Image 1](/uploads/1.1 Pathogenesis_s75_img1.png) ![Diagram: Slide 75 Image 2](/uploads/1.1 Pathogenesis_s75_img2.png) --- ## Slide 76 Dissemination The Spread of the pathogen from the sources of the pathogen to the healthy plant is called dissemination. ![Diagram: Slide 76 Image 1](/uploads/1.1 Pathogenesis_s76_img1.png) --- ## Slide 77 Dissemination by air Fungal spores and seeds The spores land or are washed out by rain Spores can be carried from several to several hundred kilometers (high altitude) This can cause an epidemic over several years. To stop this, wind break may be used. Bacteria and nematodes may be carried by wind, water, etc. --- ## Slide 78 Wind as a means of dispersal Short distance dissemination Sporangia of downy mildew fungi, conidia of powdery mildew fungi and basidiospores of rust fungi Long Distance Uredospores of rust fungi, Chlamydospores of smut fungi and conidia of Alternaria, Helminthosporium and Pyricularia, --- ## Slide 79 Dissemination by air Bacterial fire blight exudes Nematodes and spores with ground debris Windy rain Touching plants ![Diagram: Slide 79 Image 1](/uploads/1.1 Pathogenesis_s79_img1.png) --- ## Slide 80 Dissemination by water Bacteria, nematodes, spores, mycelium parts in the soil All Bacteria and many spores are exuded in a sticky liquid Rain drops or drops from overhead irrigation --- ## Slide 81 Dissemination by Insects, Mites, and Other Vectors Aphids, leafhoppers, whiteflies. Leafhoppers: mollicutes, fastidious bacteria, and protozoa. Dutch Elm disease and bacterial wilt of cucurbits. Soft rot, anthracnose, and ergot are carried by insects externally. ![Diagram: Slide 81 Image 1](/uploads/1.1 Pathogenesis_s81_img1.jpg) --- ## Slide 82 Dissemination by Insects, Mites, and other Vectors Mites and nematodes can also carry virus pathogens internally and bacteria and fungus externally Animals that walk among plants also carry Parasitic plants can carry as they bridge ![Diagram: Slide 82 Image 1](/uploads/1.1 Pathogenesis_s82_img1.jpg) --- ## Slide 83 Dissemination by Seed, Transplants, Budwood, and Nursery Stock During propagation The grower can infect his own stock and sell it out to garden centers, home owners etc. Ex: Crown gall --- ## Slide 84 Dissemination by Humans Handling diseased stock Tobacco mosaic is transmitted through cigarettes, shoes, hands Traveling long distances Tools carry pathogens from plant to plant Fireblight is a good example --- ## Slide 85 Dissemination by Humans Dutch elm disease fungus White pine blister rust fungus Citrus canker bacterium Powdery and downy mildew fungus of grape (Europe) --- ---
# Lecture: 1.2 Toxin and Host Defense # Course Slides: 1.2 Toxin ## Slide 1 The Effect Of Toxins in Plant Tissue ![Diagram: Slide 1 Image 1](/uploads/1.2 Toxin_s1_img1.png) ![Diagram: Slide 1 Image 2](/uploads/1.2 Toxin_s1_img2.png) ![Diagram: Slide 1 Image 3](/uploads/1.2 Toxin_s1_img3.jpg) --- ## Slide 2 Toxin Toxins are injurious/toxic substances secreted by micro-organism host complex that acts on living host protoplast to affect disease symptoms. **Death of cells : De Bary in 1886 **Oxalic acid secreted by Sclerotinia spp. Killing of cells. Any substance produced by a pathogen, that is injurious to the host and plays a crucial role in pathogenesis is called toxin. Toxins involved in plant parasite interactions are also called aggression. **Chemical Weapon of Pathogen** These are simple molecules with low molecular weight, they are extremely mobile and easily reach the sub cellular level of the host. Q --- ## Slide 3 ![Diagram: Slide 3 Image 1](/uploads/1.2 Toxin_s3_img1.jpg) Q --- ## Slide 4 Classification Exo-Toxins Endo-Toxins Toxins Role of Pathogenesis Specificity to Host Vivo toxins (Diamond & Waggoner, 1953) Pathotoxins (Wheeler and Luke, 1963) Phytotoxins Role is suspected in disease Alternaric aid; cochliobolin. Causal role in the disease Tab-toxin; HMT toxin Partial role in disease Fusaric acid Pyricularin Tab toxin; phseolotoxin, Tentoxin Cercosporin Victorin T-toxin, HC-toxin, AAL-toxin --- ## Slide 5 Q Types of toxin in Pathogenesis Vivotoxin A vivotoxin is defined as a substance produced in the infected host by the pathogen and/or its host, which functions in the production of disease but is not itself the initial inciting agent of disease. Characteristics of vivotoxin: Reproducible separation of the toxin from the sick plant Purification or chemical characterization Induction of at least a portion of the disease symptoms by placing the toxin in a healthy plant. Typical examples are fusaric acid and pyricularin. --- ## Slide 6 Q Types of toxin in Pathogenesis Pathotoxin: A chemical of biological origin. Host specific toxins that produces almost all the disease symptoms in absence of causal organism. Eg. T toxin, Victorin, Myrticin, HC toxin etc. Phytoalexins in plants: Low molecular weight antimicrobial compounds that are produced by plants as a response to biotic and abiotic stresses. Take part in an intricate defense system which enables plants to control invading microorganisms. --- ## Slide 7 Victorine is a host specific toxin Q ![Diagram: Slide 7 Image 1](/uploads/1.2 Toxin_s7_img1.png) --- ## Slide 8 Q Phytotoxins: Phytotoxins are poisonous substances Toxins synthesized by plants or plant pathogens through naturally occurring biochemical reactions. Plants use them as defensive agents against predators (pests, insects, stress, etc.) and diseases --- ## Slide 9 Q ![Diagram: Slide 9 Image 1](/uploads/1.2 Toxin_s9_img1.png) --- ## Slide 10 Q Effect of Toxins on Plant Tissue mechanisms of phyto-toxicity --- ## Slide 11 Effect of Toxins on Plant Tissue Changes in Cell Wall Permeability --- ## Slide 12 Changes in cell wall permeability: Toxins kill plant cells by altering the permeability of the plasma membrane thus permitting Loss of water and The electrolyte leakage/ loss of electrolytes and unrestricted entry of substances including the toxin. Na+, Ca2+, K+, Cl-, PO₄³⁻, and Mg2+ The electrolyte leakage is ubiquitous among different species, tissues, and cell types, and can be triggered by all major stress factors, including pathogen attack (Atkinson et al., 1985, 1990, 1996; Ebel and Mithofer, 1998; Blatt et al., 1999; Maffei et al., 2007) --- ## Slide 13 ![Diagram: Slide 13 Image 1](/uploads/1.2 Toxin_s13_img1.jpg) Toxins probably either destroy them or interfere with the mechanisms involved in their synthesis. **Affect cellular transport system of H+/K+ exchange in cell membrane. DEATH For example: Lycomarasmin, fusaric acid, picolinic acid, victorin und other toxins. Victorin can cause increased permeability at very low concentrations. --- ## Slide 14 Effect of Toxins on Plant Tissue 2. Disruption Of Normal Metabolic Processes --- ## Slide 15 Disruption of normal metabolic processes Role of Potassium Ion (K+) catalyzes chemical reactions by regulating > 60 enzymes acts as a catalyst by activating enzymes involved in regulating the rate of photosynthesis and the production of ATP. regulate electrical charge, which is important to maximize ATP production during photosynthesis. Regulates Stomata Opening & Closing Regulates Stomata Opening & Closing K + --- ## Slide 16 Disruption of normal metabolic processes --- ## Slide 17 Effect of Toxins on Plant Tissue 3. Malfunctioning of Enzyme System --- ## Slide 18 Tabtoxin, Phaseolotoxin and Tentoxin Inhibit normal host enzymes, thereby leading to increases in toxic substrates or to depletion of needed compounds ![Diagram: Slide 18 Image 1](/uploads/1.2 Toxin_s18_img1.jpg) ![Diagram: Slide 18 Image 2](/uploads/1.2 Toxin_s18_img2.png) ![Diagram: Slide 18 Image 3](/uploads/1.2 Toxin_s18_img3.png) Tabtoxin Phaseolotoxin Tentoxin --- ## Slide 19 Tabtoxin: Dipeptide compound of amino acid threonine and tabotoxinine Tabotoxinine Active form Toxic to cell It inactivate the enzyme glutamine synthetase ![Diagram: Slide 19 Image 1](/uploads/1.2 Toxin_s19_img1.png) --- ## Slide 20 Q Some non-toxic chemicals can become toxic due to their conversion into active toxins inside the plant. This process typically involves enzymatic reactions that occur within the host plant. Tabtoxin is a classic example of this mechanism. Tabtoxin and Its Toxicity Mechanism Produced by Pathogen: The bacterium Pseudomonas syringae pv. tabaci produces tabtoxin, a non-toxic precursor molecule. Tabtoxin itself is not harmful to plants in its original form. Structure of Tabtoxin: Tabtoxin consists of tabtoxinine-β-lactam linked to a dipeptide structure. This structure allows it to remain non-toxic initially. --- ## Slide 21 Q Activation in the Host: Once tabtoxin enters the plant cells, plant enzymes break it down, releasing the active component called tabtoxinine-β-lactam. This breakdown process occurs naturally within plant metabolism. Toxic Effect: Tabtoxinine-β-lactam inhibits the enzyme glutamine synthetase. This inhibition prevents the conversion of ammonia (NH₃) to glutamine, causing a toxic build-up of ammonia in plant tissues. --- ## Slide 22 After Inactivation of Glutamine Syntheses: Depleted glutamine levels Accumulation of toxic concentrations of ammonia Uncouples photosynthesis and photorespiration Destroy the thylakoid membrane of the chloroplast Chlorosis and necrosis --- ## Slide 23 Phaseolotoxin: The toxin that affects cells by binding to the active site and inactivating the enzyme ornithine carbamoyl transferase. Accumulate ornithine Depleted levels of citrulline and arginine --- ## Slide 24 Tentoxin: Inhibits the activity of polyphenol oxidases, enzymes involved in several resistance mechanisms of plant. Other examples: Pyricularin inhibits polyphenol oxidase system and Victorin acts by uncoupling of oxidative phosphorylation. Fusaric acid and picolinic acid can form chelating complexes with heavy metal ions and could inhibit enzyme systems which require these metals for their activity. --- ## Slide 25 Effect of Toxins on Plant Tissue 4. Interfere with the growth regulatory system --- ## Slide 26 Interference of Toxins Cause an imbalance in the hormonal system and bring about abnormal growth responses Overgrowths, rosetting, excessive root branching, stem malformation, leaf epinasty, defoliation, and suppression of bud growth. --- ## Slide 27 ![Diagram: Slide 27 Image 1](/uploads/1.2 Toxin_s27_img1.jpg) --- ## Slide 28 Plant growth regulator GA: Identified as a fungal toxin First identified in Japan in 1926, as a metabolic by product of the plant pathogen Gibberella fujikuroi, which affect rice. --- ## Slide 29 Effect of Toxins on Plant Tissue 5. Inhibit Root Growth Fusarium moniliforme produces a thermostable toxin even in soil around the roots which causes browning of roots and restrict their development. ![Diagram: Slide 29 Image 1](/uploads/1.2 Toxin_s29_img1.jpg) ![Diagram: Slide 29 Image 2](/uploads/1.2 Toxin_s29_img2.jpg) Figure: Wilt Disease of Soyabean. Besides, Stomatal Dysfunction has also been reported for certain pathotoxins Physical blocking effect of large molecules is also a mechanism of phytotoxicity by toxins. --- ## Slide 30 Causes of toxin in host plant Toxins are microbial metabolites involved in development of plant diseases. Toxins are the weapon of microbes, that helps in injuring and killing the host cell. They cause severe damage to the plant and produce different symptoms. It inhibits enzymatic reactions of the host. It is effective at low concentration and directly act upon the host protoplast. --- ---
# Lecture: 1.3 Effects of Pathogens on Plant Physiological Functions # Course Slides: 1.3 Effects of Pathogens on Plant Physiological Functions ## Slide 1 Effects of pathogens on plant physiological functions --- ## Slide 2 Plant Physiology: Plant physiology is the study of plant processes It includes many processes like : Photosynthesis Respiration translocation of water, minerals Transpiration ![Diagram: Slide 2 Image 1](/uploads/1.3 Effects of Pathogens on Plant Physiological Functions_s2_img1.jpg) --- ## Slide 3 Photosynthesis Photosynthesis is the basic function of green plants: It enables them to transform light energy into chemical energy, which they can utilize in all cell activities. Photosynthesis is the ultimate source of nearly all energy used in all living cells, plant or animal --- ## Slide 4 Q Effects of pathogens on photosynthesis How do we tell that photosynthesis has been affected? Pathogens damage leaf tissues – reduced photosynthetic area examples of such diseases Some diseases destroy chloroplasts and cause their disintegration Pathogens produce toxins such as tentoxin and tabtoxin which inhibit enzymes involved in photosynthesis Vascular pathogens reduce chlorophyll content and photosynthesis stops before wilting Viruses and mollicute cause chlorosis and stunting – photosynthesis is greatly reduced --- ## Slide 5 ![Diagram: Slide 5 Image 1](/uploads/1.3 Effects of Pathogens on Plant Physiological Functions_s5_img1.jpg) ![Diagram: Slide 5 Image 2](/uploads/1.3 Effects of Pathogens on Plant Physiological Functions_s5_img2.jpg) Spot with necrosis Blight --- ## Slide 6 Effect on translocation of water and nutrients uptake Vascular pathogens affect movement of water from the roots Vascular pathogens also affect movement of nutrients from soil to the leaves – rest of plant deprived of nutrients If water to leaves is inhibited, leaves cannot function properly – photosynthesis reduced or stopped Some pathogens affect the integrity or function of the roots Other interfere with water economy by causing excessive transpiration through leaves and stomata Q --- ## Slide 7 Diseases caused by Oomycetes Seed rots and Seedling Damping-off ![Diagram: Slide 7 Image 1](/uploads/1.3 Effects of Pathogens on Plant Physiological Functions_s7_img1.png) --- ## Slide 8 ![Diagram: Slide 8 Image 1](/uploads/1.3 Effects of Pathogens on Plant Physiological Functions_s8_img1.jpg) Damping-off    Death of a seedling before or shortly after emergence due to decomposition of the root and/or lower stem; it is common to distinguish between preemergence damping-off and postemergence damping-off (Pythium damping-off of verbena) --- ## Slide 9 Q Interfere with translocation in xylem vessels Physical presence (mycelium, conidia, bacterial cells) in xylem Polysaccharides in the vessels Collapse of vessels Development of tyloses. Reduced water tension in vessels due to pathogen-induces alteration in foliar transpiration --- ## Slide 10 ![Diagram: Slide 10 Image 1](/uploads/1.3 Effects of Pathogens on Plant Physiological Functions_s10_img1.jpg) Bean root rot --- ## Slide 11 ![Diagram: Slide 11 Image 1](/uploads/1.3 Effects of Pathogens on Plant Physiological Functions_s11_img1.png) ![Diagram: Slide 11 Image 2](/uploads/1.3 Effects of Pathogens on Plant Physiological Functions_s11_img2.png) Wilt:      Drooping of leaves and stems from lack of water (inadequate water supply or excessive transpiration);vascular disease that interrupts normal water uptake. --- ## Slide 12 Root and Crown Rot ![Diagram: Slide 12 Image 1](/uploads/1.3 Effects of Pathogens on Plant Physiological Functions_s12_img1.jpg) --- ## Slide 13 ![Diagram: Slide 13 Image 1](/uploads/1.3 Effects of Pathogens on Plant Physiological Functions_s13_img1.jpg) Canker The death of cambium tissue by the formation of sharply delineated, dry, necrotic, localized lesions on the stem --- ## Slide 14 Q Translocation of nutrients through phloem Pathogens may interfere with movement of nutrients from leaf cells to phloem. Rusts and mildews cause accumulation of photosynthetic products in areas invaded by pathogens Infected areas characterized by reduced phosynthesis and increased respiration Canker causes pathogens to attack the stem and remain confined there. Pathogen may attack and destroy phloem elements – interfere with downward translocation of nutrients. --- ## Slide 15 Dieback: Progressive death of shoots and twigs generally starting at the tip of the infected plant part ![Diagram: Slide 15 Image 1](/uploads/1.3 Effects of Pathogens on Plant Physiological Functions_s15_img1.jpg) --- ## Slide 16 ![Diagram: Slide 16 Image 1](/uploads/1.3 Effects of Pathogens on Plant Physiological Functions_s16_img1.jpg) Downy mildew:   A plant disease in which the fungus appears as a downy growth on the host surface; caused by a member of the Oomycetes. --- ## Slide 17 ![Diagram: Slide 17 Image 1](/uploads/1.3 Effects of Pathogens on Plant Physiological Functions_s17_img1.jpg) powdery mildew powdery mildew powdery mildew powdery mildew Powdery mildew White, powdery, superficial ascomycetous fungus that is an obligate parasite and generally is found only on one or a few closely related species of plants --- ## Slide 18 ![Diagram: Slide 18 Image 1](/uploads/1.3 Effects of Pathogens on Plant Physiological Functions_s18_img1.jpg) Rust:    A disease caused by a specialized group of Basidiomycetes that often produces spores of a rusty color (stem rust of wheat) --- ## Slide 19 Effect of pathogens on host plant respiration Rate of respiration higher in infected plants Infected tissues use up reserve carbohydrates faster than healthy tissues Respirations higher in infected resistant cultivars – higher energy required for production and mobilization of defense mechanisms Increased respiration has been noticed in cereal rusts, powdery mildews, blast of rice, late blight of potato and many other diseases. Level of many enzymes associated with respiratory processes is increased in diseased plants. Diseased plants carry out more fermentation than the healthy plants. --- ## Slide 20 ![Diagram: Slide 20 Image 1](/uploads/1.3 Effects of Pathogens on Plant Physiological Functions_s20_img1.jpg) --- ## Slide 21 Effect on permeability of cell membranes Cell membrane permeability is altered Uncontrolled loss of electrolytes, water soluble ions Inflow of excess undesirable substances --- ## Slide 22 Effect on Transpiration Transpiration is the process of water movement through the plant and its evaporation from aerial parts, such as leaves, stem and flowers. It is normally observed that the rate of transpiration increases in plants in which the plant diseases infect the leaves (e.g., leaf-rusts, apple scab, powdery mildews, and downy mildews). It happens because these diseases result in destruction of the part of the protection devices like cuticle, an increase in permeability of leaf cells, and the dysfunction of stomata. --- ## Slide 23 Transpiration Increases when plant pathogens infect leaves because of an increase in leaf cell permeability and stomata dysfunction. Destruction of a considerable portion of the cuticle and epidermis results in an uncontrolled loss of water from the affected areas. If water absorption and translocation cannot keep up with excessive loss of water, loss of turgor and wilting of leaves. --- ## Slide 24 Effects of pathogens on transpiration Disintegeration of cuticle. Collapse of vessels. Formation of tyloses. Powdery mildew- Epidermal cells invaded by the fungus. Rust- Leaf surface get exposed due to rupture of epidermis cause unrestricted loss of water. Blight- Number of active and healthy cells reduced, transport of water to leaves by xylem is reduced. --- ---
# Lecture: 2.1 Dissemination of Pathogens # Course Slides: 2.1 Dessimination of the Pathogen ## Slide 1 Q Pathogen: An entity that can incite disease. Inoculum: The pathogen or its parts that can cause infection; that portion of individual pathogens that are brought into contact with the host. --- ## Slide 2 Kinds of inoculum produced by pathogens Plant pathogenic organisms produce several kinds of inocula- Fungi: In case of fungi different kinds of inocula are produced- Different kinds of spores/Conidia: Ascospore, Basidiospore, Teliospore, Pycnidiospore --- ## Slide 3 The growing mycelium. e.g. soil-borne pathogens The dormant mycelium in seeds or in often plant parts e.g. late blight pathogen in tubers, loose smut pathogen in seeds Mycelial strands or rhizomorph Sclerotia: Sclerotia are of various sizes and shapes. e.g. Sclerotium rolfsii, Rhizoctonia solani --- ## Slide 4 Virus and Bacteria No special inoculum is produced by virus and bacteria (except the genus Streptomyces, it produces spores). The individual virus particle and the individual bacterial cell constitute the inoculum. Nematodes In case of nematodes either the organism itself or its egg act as inoculum. Parasitic flowering plants Seeds of parasitic higher plants act as inoculum. --- ## Slide 5 Dissemination/dispersal/transmission transport of spores or infectious bodies, acting as inoculum, from one host to another host at various distances resulting in the spread of the disease Displacement of a plant pathogen from its place of production or origin to a suitable place where it can grow/establish --- ## Slide 6 EFFECTIVE DISPERSAL If displacement occurs on a susceptible host and where the environment is suitable, the pathogen can grow and multiply Ineffective Dispersal When plant pathogen does not get any medium for its survival. For example, if it falls on a stone --- ## Slide 7 The dispersal of infectious plant pathogens in space occurs through two ways: Autonomous or direct or active dispersal In this method the dispersal of plant pathogens takes place through soil, seed and planting material during normal agronomic operations. There is no major role of external agencies like insects, wind, water, etc. in this type of dispersal. Indirect or passive dispersal. in this type of dispersal insects, wind ,water, Human etc. --- ## Slide 8 Q What is dissemination? Displacement of a plant pathogen from its place of production/origin to a suitable place where it can grow/establish. Transport of spores or infectious bodies, acting as inoculum, from one host to another host at various distances resulting in the spread of the disease. --- ## Slide 9 Q Dissemination Ineffective When the displaced plant pathogen doesn’t get any medium for its survival. Effective When the displacement occurs on susceptible host and where the environment is suitable for the displaced pathogen to establish infection. --- ## Slide 10 Q Why Pathogen Disseminate (Importance) Food and nutrition Survival Complete life cycle Reproduction --- ## Slide 11 Types of dissemination of plant pathogens Indirect Dissemination Direct Dissemination through seed or other planting materials. External The spores of Ustilago hordei are transmitted through adherence to the seed surface Internal Passive By means of external agents Active/ autonomous dispersal By growth or mobility of pathogens Vegetative Phytophthora infestans causes late blight of potato Germinitive Ustilago tritici (Loose smut of wheat) --- ## Slide 12 Q Agents of dissemination Wind Water Soil Seed Animal Human Machinery used in agriculture Transport system --- ## Slide 13 Wind dissemination --- ## Slide 14 Characteristics of Wind disseminating fungi Numerous spore discharge of spores with sufficient force Light in weight smaller size Thick outer wall Less affinity to water  E.g. Most fungi except synchytriaceae, Plasmodiophoromyces, Fusarium Bacteria X. malvacearum, Erwinia amylovora Nematode Anguina tritici, Heterodera --- ## Slide 15 Wind as a means of dispersal Short distance dissemination sporangia of downy mildew fungi, conidia of powdery mildew fungi and basidiospores of rust fungi Long distance Uredospores of rust fungi, Chlamydospores of smut fungi and conidia of Alternaria, Helminthosporium and Pyricularia --- ## Slide 16 Factors affecting wind dissemination Periodicity of wind  Diurnal Nocturnal Air current Steady-horizontal Breeze & turbulance-Vertical Irregular-Multidimensional --- ## Slide 17 Some Example P. Graminis tritici Horizontal---600Km Altitude-------500m above of the infected field P. Infestans- Horizontal---200m to 600Km Spores of Molds Aspergillus Penicillium Cladosporium   Altitude----------2000m above --- ## Slide 18 Dispersal by water Fungi dissemination Surface running water  after heavy rains during irrigation: canals and wells Ex: The mycelial fragments, spores or sclerotia of fungi, Colletotrichum falcatum, Fusarium, Ganoderma, Macrophomina, Pythium, Phytophthora, Sclerotium, etc. Long distance ------ floods --- ## Slide 19 By rain splash Most efficient means for bacteria. Rain drops falling with force on sori, pustules, cankers or even soil surface may splash the propagules ---in small droplets and enable them to land on neighboring healthy susceptible surfaces or  ---the water droplets may be carried to long distances by air. --- ## Slide 20 Ex: Bacterial leaf spot of rice (Xanthomonas campestris pv. oryzae),Bacterial leaf streak of rice (Xanthomonas campestris pv. oryzicola) Fungal spores and bacteria present in the air or plant surface are washed downward by rain splash or drops from overhead irrigation and are deposited on susceptible healthy plants. --- ## Slide 21 Insects dissemination   Fungal pathogens honey secretions having attractive odours. Ex: Sugary disease of sorghum, ergot of rai Bacterial pathogens Cucumber wilt- E. tracheiphila ------stripped cucumber beetles (Acalymma vittata) --- ## Slide 22 Viral pathogens More than 80 % of viral and phytoplasma diseases Largest family- Homoptera- Aphids and leaf hoppers mealy bugs and scale insects (Coccoidae) whiteflies (Aleurodidae) hoppers (Membracidae) in Homoptera also transmit virus diseases --- ## Slide 23 Insects dissemination Mechanical Fungi-----------------------------Legs Bacteria--------------------------Bristles Viruses---------------------------Wings Mycoplasma--------------------Abdomen --- ## Slide 24 Specificity of Insect Transmission  Aphids----------Mosaic group---------Tomato mosaic, Papaya mosaic, potato leaf roll, chili mosaic Leaf Hopper---------Yellows ---------------Tungro of rice Thrips---Spots with concentric ring----Pineapple yellow spots Whiteflies------Thickening of veins/veinlets------Tobacco leaf curl, Tomato leaf curl --- ## Slide 25 Dispersal of plant pathogens by insects Viruses different insects sucking and chewing or biting type mouth parts Hemoptera. two families: Aphidae (aphids) Cicadellidae (Leaf hoppers) . A few belong to the aleyroidae (whiteflies) and the coccidae (mealy bugs). --- ## Slide 26 More than fifty species of aphids are known to transmit the viruses of plants. Myzus persicae alone can transmit more than fifty species. Aphids: bean common mosaic virus, bunchy top mycoplasma, potato leaf roll virus, chilli and tomato mosaic. White flies: leaf curl of tomato and papaya. Tungro virus is transmitted by leaf hoppers and brown plant hoppers. --- ## Slide 27 The transmission of plant viruses by insects may be by two ways: Mechanical : When the viruses are simply carried externally or internally by the insects. Insects have biting type of mouth parts e.g. coleoptera beetles squash mosaic, cowpea mosaic, turnip yellow mosaic --- ## Slide 28 2. Biological: When the pathogens are transmitted through sexual process from generation to generation. Insects having sucking type of mouth parts such as aphids, leafhoppers, whiteflies, mealy bugs. --- ## Slide 29 Biological transmission are of three types: a. Non-persistent (or) stylet-borne: Viruses requiring no incubation time e.g. Potato virus Y b. Semi-persistent (or) foregut-borne c. Persistent : Viruses requiring incubation time e .g. Potato leaf roll virus - circulative (no virus replication) - circulative-propagative (virus replication) --- ## Slide 30 Non- and Semi-persistent transmission • virus is not internalized in the vector • virus do not enter the hemocoel and salivary glands of the vector Persistent circulative transmission • virus is internalized in the vector • virus enters the hemocoel and salivary glands of the vector --- ## Slide 31 Persistent circulative-propagative transmission • virus is internalized in the vector • virus enters the hemocoel and salivary glands of the vector • virus multiplies in the insect vector --- ## Slide 32 ![Diagram: Slide 32 Image 1](/uploads/2.1 Dessimination of the Pathogen_s32_img1.wmf) --- ## Slide 33 Fungi transmitted by insects: About 66 species of fungi causing plant diseases are transmitted by more than a 100 species of insects belonging to at least six orders. Transmission occurs in various ways with or without injury to the plant. --- ## Slide 34 For example, spores of Ustilago spp and Botrytis spp are carried from the infected anthers to the uninfected flowers by pollinating insects such as Bees, wasps. Aphids can carry the mycelial fragments and sporangia of Phytophthora infestans on their bodies and leg for the short distance dissemination. --- ## Slide 35 Bacteria transmitted by insects: For example, Cucumber beetle chew the plant parts infected by cucurbit wilt pathogen, Erwinia tracheiphila and carry the bacterium inside the body and passing on the host in the next season Seed corm maggot can disseminate the black leg pathogen, Pectobacterium carotovora susp carotovora. Xanthomonas axonopodis pv. citri (citrus canker pathogen) is disseminated by citrus leaf miner. Mycoplasma transmitted by insects: Such as Psyllid bug transmits citrus greening mycoplasma and whitely transmits little leaf mycoplasma. --- ## Slide 36 Dispersal or transmission of plant pathogens by Seed Most effective and the easiest way of transmission Approximately 500 plant species are attacked by seed-borne pathogens. Each of the cereal crops rice, wheat, sorghum and maize may be attacked by nearly 500 different seed-borne pathogens. It appears that as many as 41 seed-borne pathogens occur on bean and 3 dozens of different pathogens are transmitted through soybean seeds. --- ## Slide 37 Example: Many seed-borne pathogens have worldwide distribution and under favourable conditions they may cause disease in epidemic form. Fungi Loose smut of wheat (Ustilago tritici) Leaf blight of wheat (Bipolaris sorokiniama) Blast of rice (Pyricularia oryzae) Brown spot of rice (Bipolaris oryzae) --- ## Slide 38 Bacteria Erwinia carotovora (Soft rot of potato) Xanthomonas oryzae pv. oryzae (Leaf blight of rice) Common bacterial blight of bean (Xanthomonas phaseoli) Nematodes Ditylenchus angustus (Ufra of rice) Aphelenchoides besseyi (White tip of rice) Virus Bean common mosaic virus Papaya leaf curl virus --- ## Slide 39 Mechanism of seed transmission: Pathogens may exist with the seeds in two ways: Infection Contamination | Types of association | Parts infected/ contaminated | Category of infection/ contamination | |---|---|---| | Seed infection | Embryo | Embryo infection | | | Endosperm, seed coat/ pericarp | Extra-embryal infection | | Seed contamination | Seed coat/ Pericarp | Contamination | | | Bract, glume etc. | | --- ## Slide 40 Dispersal of plant pathogens by nematodes Some viruses as well as some bacterial and fungal plant pathogens are transmitted by nematodes. Examples: Corynebacterium fascians is disseminated by ectoparasitic nematodes, Aphelenchoides Yellow ear rot pathogen of wheat, Corynebacterium tritici is dispersed by the ear cockle nematode, Anguina tritici. --- ## Slide 41 Fungi There is evidence that some root infecting pathogens, particularly fungi, may enter roots through wounds made by nematodes. Such as cotton wilt caused by Fusarium oxysporum f. sp. vasinfectum, the pathogen being associated with root knots. --- ## Slide 42 Bacteria Bacterial wilt caused by Ralstonia solanacearum is more severe with the presence of root knot nematodes which probably facilitate the entry of the bacteria thus help in disseminating the pathogens. --- ## Slide 43 Viruses The grapevine fan leaf virus is disseminated by Xiphinema index. Some polyhedral and tubular viruses have also been shown to be nematode transmitted, the former by the species of Xiphinema, Longidorus and Trichodorus spp. --- ## Slide 44 Dispersal of plant pathogens by human one of the important agents. short and long distance. day to day activities Through propagating materials propagation field to field, orchard to orchard and from one geographic region to another. --- ## Slide 45 ii. Through import and export of seeds and other propagating materials Xanthomonas oryzae pv. oryzicola (bacterial leaf streak pathogen), potato viruses, Ustilago nuda (loose smut of wheat), Sphacelotheca sorghi (grain smut of jawar), Neovossia indica (kernal bunt of wheat) Anguina tritici (ear cockle of wheat). --- ## Slide 46 iii. Movement of farm workers: Spores and other vegetative and reproduction structures (sclerotia etc) can be carried by worker clothings, shoes, hands etc. from plant to plant and from field to field. --- ## Slide 47 Examples: Angular leaf spot of cucurbits caused by Pseudomonas lachrymans. When cucumbers are grown for pickles, the fruits are picked. When a person comes in contact with or picks an infected plants, his hands become contaminated , particularly when the plants are wet with dew. The pathogen, is thus, spread down the row of the plants. --- ## Slide 48 The virus that are spread by animals can also be spread by man. Tobacco Mosaic Virus is known to survive in dry tobacco leaves. Hence, workers who handle tobacco plants in the field are advised not to smoke while they are working and to thoroughly wash their hands before entering the field. --- ## Slide 49 iv. Use of contaminated implements: cutting knives etc. helps in transmission of soil-borne pathogens and also the pathogens present in bulbs, tubers, sugarcane setts etc. v. Through grafting and budding between healthy and diseased plants: It is the most effective method of distribution of pathogens of fruit trees such as the careless selection of stock and scion for propagating citrus trees. --- ## Slide 50 vi. Cultural operations: Hoeing, weeding, pruning carried out by man not only transmit diseases by contact between the plant and contaminated tools but also by providing wounds on plant surface for entry of pathogens that have no capacity to cause direct penetration --- ## Slide 51 Dispersal by phanerogamic parasites: Phanerogamic parasites transmit the viruses by acting as a bridge between the diseased and healthy plants. Ex: Dodder (Cuscuta California, C. campesris, C. subinclusaetc.) Cuscuta subinclusa----Cucumber mosaic virus Cuscuta california -----Tobacco mosaic virus, Tobacco rattlevirus, Tomato spotted wilt virus Cuscuta campestris - Tomato bushy stunt virus --- ## Slide 52 Dispersal by animals: Cattle feeding on diseased fodder ingest the viable fungal propagules (spores or oospores or sclerotia) and pass out as such in the dung. Soil inhabiting fungi especially sclerotia adhere to the hoofs and legs of animals and get transported to other places. --- ## Slide 53 Dispersal by other mammals and birds: Pathogens that can persist on clothing or machinery might easily be carried on an animals hair or a birds feathers. Examples: Rabbits and dogs spread potato virus x in potato crops when they brush past infected plants and break the leaf hairs. Birds carry the chestnut blight fungus (Endothia parasitica). Birds also spread the seeds of parasitic higher plants (dodder) --- ---
# Lecture: 2.2 Epidemiology of Plant Diseases # Course Slides: 2.2 Epidemiology ## Slide 1 What is Plant Pathology or Phytopathology? Pathology comes from 2 Greek words Pathos Suffering Logos Study Plant Pathology or Phytopathology is the study of suffering of plant diseases. --- ## Slide 2 Why do we study Plant Pathology? To study the causes of diseases To study the interaction(s) between the plant and the pathogen To study the mechanism(s) of disease development. To develop management strategy of the diseases --- ## Slide 3 Disease = Disturbance from plant pathogen or environmental factor that interferes with plant physiology Causes changes in plant appearance or yield loss Disease results from: Direct damage to cells Toxins, growth regulators, or other byproducts that affect metabolism Use of nutrients and water or interference with their uptake --- ## Slide 4 Epidemic Gr. Epi=upon, among and Demons=people Epidemic What is among people "Change in disease intensity in a host population over time and space." Change: often increase -- a dynamic process Disease: dealing with diseases, not just the pathogen (or plant/crop) Host: Organism infected (or potentially infected) by another organism Population: a population phenomenon Time and space: two physical dimensions of interest. --- ## Slide 5 Q Epidemic and Epidemiology When a pathogen spreads to and affects many individuals within a population over a relatively large area and within a relatively short time, the phenomenon is called an epidemic. An epidemic has been defined as any increase of disease in a population. A similar definition of an epidemic is the dynamics of change in plant disease in time and space. --- ## Slide 6 Plant Disease Epidemiology Study of epidemics The study of epidemics and of the factors that influence them is called epidemiology. Epidemiology is concerned simultaneously with populations of pathogens and host plants as they occur in an evolving environment and human interferences. Edpidemiology or epiphytology is the study of the outbreak of disease, its course, intensity, cause, effects and the various factors governing it. --- ## Slide 7 History (ancient to modern times) Hippocrates (~400 BC): First use of "epidemic", widespread disease (human diseases) Theophrastus (~340 BC): Plant diseases in fields, Environmental influences Pliny (~50 AD): Plant diseases; soil; climate Duhamel de Monceau (1728 AD): Disease progress curves, Comparison of plant and animal epidemics Late 19th Century and forward…0 Kuhn (1858) - 1st textbook of plant pathology Ward (1901): book "Diseases in Plants" emphasized ecology (populations) of disease Jones (1913) - role of the environment Gaumann (1946): "Principles of Plant Infection” -Disease spread, -Conditions leading to an epidemic, -'Infection Chain' (= disease cycle), -compare with medicine (diseases of humans) Disease 7 --- ## Slide 8 Vanderplank (1963) (used to be van der Plank) "Plant Diseases: Epidemics and Control" -Populations -Rates (dynamic processes) -Analysis, mathematics -Models, theory -Link epidemiology and control -Established the science of plant disease epidemiology Other pioneers: Zadoks (1960-1995), The Netherlands Kranz (1968-1995), Germany Waggoner (1960-mid --1980s), USA S. Nagaranjan 1983-India Note: many developments in other fields… Ecology, medical epidemiology, Biomathematics, etc. Large (1952, and others) -Disease progress curves -Crop losses -Disease assessment (measurement) Horsfall & Dimond (1960)- "Plant Pathology, Volume 3" -Populations -Inoculum density: disease relations -Spore dispersal -Analysis (mathematics) -Forecasting, prediction -Traditional definition ---> Modern definition Gregory (1963, 1973) "The Microbiology of the Atmosphere" -spore dispersal, disease spread Aerobiology 8 --- ## Slide 9 Q Based on the occurrence and geographical distribution they are classified as follows: 1. Endemic or Enphytotic When a disease is more or less constantly occurring year after year in a moderate to severe form in a country or locality then it is called as endemic disease. eg: wart disease of potato (Synchytrium endobioticum) is endemic in Darjeeling, citrus canker (Xanthomonas axonopodis pv citri)in Asia and sorghum rust (Puccinia purpurea) Certain disease are endemic in one area and become epidemic in another area. Eg: Citrus canker is endemic in Asia but epidemic in the introduced place, Florida (U.S.A).The downy mildew of corn is a endemic disease in India but became epidemic in the Philippine --- ## Slide 10 Q 2. Epidemic or Epiphytotic It is a sudden outbreak of a disease periodically over a widespread area in a devastatingly severe form causing severe losses or complete destruction. This is constantly present in a locality but it assumes severe form only on occasions. This is because of the occurrence of favorable environment responsible for the rapid development of disease. eg: wheat stem rust (Puccinia graminis tritici), late blight of potato (Phytophthora infestans)Irish famine 1845-1846, and rice blast (Pyricularia oryzae) Bengal famine 1943. --- ## Slide 11 Q 3. Pandemic When an epidemic disease spreads over continents or subcontinents and involves mass mortality it is considered as pandemic. The outbreak of black stem rust of wheat in India during 1947 is best example for a pandemic disease. 4. Sporadic Diseases which occur at irregular intervals over limited areas or locations are called sporadic. They occur relatively in few instances. Eg: Fusarium wilt of cotton (Fusarium oxysporum f sp. vasiinfectum) and loose smut of wheat (Ustilago tritici). --- ## Slide 12 Q Vanderplank’s Equivalence Theorem “Effects of host, pathogen and environment can be translated into terms of the rate parameter of an epidemic” Changes in any component has an equivalent effect on disease -More-less susceptible host -More-less favorable environment -More-less amount of aggressive pathogen disease --- ## Slide 13 Q Therefore, disease management principles and practices are often centered around the concept of the Disease Triangle. so that management tactics often seek to manipulate one or more of the components of the disease triangle. --- ## Slide 14 Elements of an epidemics An epidemic may cause widespread and mass destruction of crop in a short time or may persist for long periods depending upon the following factors responsible for the disease: --- ## Slide 15 Q Elements of a disease cycle Disease development is also affected by 4. Time 5. Humans 1.Host 2. Pathogen 3. Environment Interactions of the 3 main components are described by the disease triangle. ![Diagram: Slide 15 Image 1](/uploads/2.2 Epidemiology_s15_img1.png) ![Diagram: Slide 15 Image 2](/uploads/2.2 Epidemiology_s15_img2.png) Interactions of the 5 components are described by the disease pyramid. The Disease Triangle --- ## Slide 16 The Disease Triangle ![Diagram: Slide 16 Image 1](/uploads/2.2 Epidemiology_s16_img1.png) --- ## Slide 17 ![Diagram: Slide 17 Image 1](/uploads/2.2 Epidemiology_s17_img1.png) Temp. Fluctuation Effects --- ## Slide 18 Factors that affect the development of epidemic --- ## Slide 19 Host factors A. Level of genetic resistance or Susceptibility of the host Susceptibility- The lack of ability to resist the effect of a pathogen or other damaging factors. Resistance- The ability of an organism to exclude or overcome pathogens completely or in some degree. a. true resistance-gene control, eg: vertical and horizontal resistance b. apparent resistance- not genetically control # disease escape- to overcome the disease developing factors # disease tolerance- disease occur but not economic loss Race : A genetically and often geographically distinct mating group within a species of a pathogen. Q --- ## Slide 20 Host factors B. Degree of genetic uniformity of the host plant Eg : Vegetative propagated crop, self-pollinated crop, monoculture, especially clones are more susceptible to diseases. Q C. Types of crop- - Annual crops & foliar or fruit diseases develop much more rapidly (in few weeks) - Perennial woody diseases take longer time to develop (in years). D. Age of crop plant – Plant change in their susceptibility to disease with age. Some plants are susceptible only during growth period & become resistant during mature period. Q --- ## Slide 21 ![Diagram: Slide 21 Image 1](/uploads/2.2 Epidemiology_s21_img1.jpg) E. Introduction of new hosts- --- ## Slide 22 Pathogenic Factors Level of virulence and aggressiveness – Virulence- Degree of pathogenecity of a given pathogen. Aggressiveness – Production of a large number of inoculums within a very short period of time. B. Quantity of inoculums near host- More quantity, more probability of causing diseases. Q --- ## Slide 23 C. Types of reproduction of the pathogen- A central concept to epidemiology is that different pathogen populations have different disease cycles. I. Monocyclic = single cycle (simple interest) Pathogens that complete one or even part of one disease cycle/year are called monocyclic In monocyclic pathogens the primary inoculum is the only inoculum available for the entire season. There is no secondary inoculum and no secondary infection. The amount of inoculum produced at the end of the season, however, is greater than at the start of the season so the amount of inoculum may increase steadily from year to year. Eg. Loose smut of wheat Q --- ## Slide 24 ![Diagram: Slide 24 Image 1](/uploads/2.2 Epidemiology_s24_img1.jpg) Loose smut of wheat --- ## Slide 25 This representation of plant disease over time is referred to as a “Disease Progress Curve” Graphically, disease caused by monocyclic pathogens looks like a saturation curve. ![Diagram: Slide 25 Image 1](/uploads/2.2 Epidemiology_s25_img1.jpg) --- ## Slide 26 Basis of modelling : All the epidemiological models are based on the cyclic pattern of disease occurrence. Monocyclic disease Polycyclic disease How to reduce disease incidence, x, at any point in the epidemic: Reduce initial inoculum, Q (monocyclic) Xo (Polycyclic). Reduce rate constant, R. Reduce duration of epidemic, t. --- ## Slide 27 ![Diagram: Slide 27 Image 1](/uploads/2.2 Epidemiology_s27_img1.wmf) The monocyclic model (Van der plank in 1963) It is clear from the above model of a monocyclic epidemic that Q, R, and t have equal weight in their effect on x. A reduction in the initial inoculum or the rate of infection will result in a reduction in the level of disease by the same proportion at any time(t), throughout the epidemic. If t can be reduced (for example, by shortening the season), disease will be reduced proportionately. --- ## Slide 28 Examining these models, we can see that in both there are three ways in which we can reduce x at any point in the epidemic: Reduce the initial inoculum (Q in the monocyclic model and xo in the polycyclic model). (Actually xo is the initial incidence of disease, which is proportional to the initial inoculum) Reduce the rate of infection (R in the monocyclic model and r in the polycyclic model) Reduce the duration of the epidemic (the time, t, at the end of the epidemic) --- ## Slide 29 Effect of xo on epidemic development: xo depends upon: – Inoculum from previous crops within a field. – Inoculum from crops in adjacent fields. xo is affected by: – By destroying infected plant parts. – By removing diseased plants. – Through chemical seed treatments. – Use of protective fungicides. – Race-specific disease resistance. – Biological control agents targeted at initial inoculum. --- ## Slide 30 Effect of r on epidemic development: r depends upon: – Reproductive potential of the pathogen. – Virulence of the pathogen. – Susceptibility of the host. – Favorability of environment. r is affected by: – Non-specific disease resistance. – Use of systemic fungicides. – Cultural practices that alter environment. – By removal of diseased plants. --- ## Slide 31 Rate of increase of disease over time can be represented by a simple interest function ![Diagram: Slide 31 Image 1](/uploads/2.2 Epidemiology_s31_img1.jpg) ![Diagram: Slide 31 Image 2](/uploads/2.2 Epidemiology_s31_img2.wmf) --- ## Slide 32 Examples of Monocyclic Diseases Blackleg of potato (Erwinia caratovora) Verticillium wilt Cereal Cyst Nematode --- ## Slide 33 Q Polycyclic = multiple cycles/year (compound interest) The pathogens that go through more than one (2-30) disease cycle in a growing season and are referred to as polycyclic. Only a small number of sexual spores or other hardy structures survive as primary inoculum that cause initial infections. --- ## Slide 34 Once infection takes place, large numbers of asexual spores are produced as secondary inoculum at each infection site. These spores can produce new (secondary) infections that produce more asexual spores and so on. With each cycle the amount of inoculum is multiplied manyfold. --- ## Slide 35 Graphically this type of population growth is represented as a sigmoid curve ![Diagram: Slide 35 Image 1](/uploads/2.2 Epidemiology_s35_img1.jpg) --- ## Slide 36 ![Diagram: Slide 36 Image 1](/uploads/2.2 Epidemiology_s36_img1.jpg) --- ## Slide 37 ![Diagram: Slide 37 Image 1](/uploads/2.2 Epidemiology_s37_img1.wmf) The polycyclic model If r is very high, the apparent effect of reducing xo is to delay the epidemic. If r is very high, xo must be reduced to very low levels to have a significant effect on the epidemic. Reducing r has a relatively greater effect on the epidemic than reducing xo. Reducing xo makes good strategic sense only if r is low or if r is also being reduced. --- ## Slide 38 ![Diagram: Slide 38 Image 1](/uploads/2.2 Epidemiology_s38_img1.jpg) Rate of increase of disease over time can be represented by a compound interest function. --- ## Slide 39 Many of these polycyclic pathogens are disseminated primarily by air or air-borne vectors and are responsible for most of the explosive epidemics in most crops Examples of Polcyclic Diseases Downy mildews Powdery mildews Late blight of potato Leaf spots Blights Grain rusts Aphid borne viruses Root-knot nematodes --- ## Slide 40 ![Diagram: Slide 40 Image 1](/uploads/2.2 Epidemiology_s40_img1.jpg) Q --- ## Slide 41 ![Diagram: Slide 41 Image 1](/uploads/2.2 Epidemiology_s41_img1.jpg) Q --- ## Slide 42 III. Polyetic (multi-year) cycles The pathogens that take several years before the inoculum they produce can be disseminated and initiate new infections are called polyetic pathogens. May not cause many new infections over a given area in a year, amount of inoculum does not increase greatly within a year. However, because they survive in perennial hosts they have almost as much inoculum as they had at the end of the previous year. Inoculum may increase steadily(exponentially) from year to year and can cause severe outbreaks when considered over several years. --- ## Slide 43 Examples of Polyetic Diseases: 1. Some diseases of trees: Dutch elm disease Citrus tristeza Fungal vascular wilts 2. Mycoplasmal yellows 3. Viral infections --- ## Slide 44 Pathogenic Factors D. Ecology of the pathogen- Presence of inoculums in the host Vascular fungi reproduce inside of the host and vector transmitted cause epidemic Soil borne pathogen produce inoculums on the infected field. E. Mode of spread of the pathogen- - Wind, Water, Soil, Seed, insect, Human, Machinery used in agriculture --- ## Slide 45 Q Environmental Factors Temperature Host effect- High temperature break the host resistance especially vertical resistance. Pathogen effect- High temperature increase the growth of pathogen but low temp. reduce the amount of inoculum also reduce the movement of vectors. --- ## Slide 46 Q Environmental Factors Moisture Activates resting stages Affects germination of spores and penetration into host eg. Soil borne pathogen produce inoculums on the infected field Water on leaves Humidity and Splashing water- distributes inoculum --- ## Slide 47 Q ![Diagram: Slide 47 Image 1](/uploads/2.2 Epidemiology_s47_img1.png) Moisture Effect of foliage density on development of Phytophthora infestans during a period of partly favorable weather (May–June) and of very favorable weather (November– December). --- ## Slide 48 Human Factors A. Site selection and preparation -Poor drainage, low land, infected field. B. Selection of propagating materials -Using pathogen free or treated planting materials reduce disease. C. Cultural practices -Monoculture, higher level of nitrogenous fertilizer, dense planting, overhead irrigation, poor sanitation, creation injury in plant. D. Disease control -Cultural , biological chemical control reduce the epidemic. --- ## Slide 49 Q Disease development is also affected by Time Time factors Season of the year Duration & frequency of favorable temp. & rains 3. Appearance of vectors, etc. --- ## Slide 50 Q Eg: Outbreak of Phytophthora wilt of betelvine occurs during rainy season in Bangladesh. This disease once again become destructive during rainy season. This type of epidemic is known as seasonal epidemic or annual epidemic. Eg: Epidemics caused as a result of introduction of new pathogens in the locality. The well known epidemics of late blight of potato in Europe and blast disease of rice in South East Asia. --- ## Slide 51 ![Diagram: Slide 51 Image 1](/uploads/2.2 Epidemiology_s51_img1.jpg) --- ## Slide 52 ![Diagram: Slide 52 Image 1](/uploads/2.2 Epidemiology_s52_img1.jpg) --- ## Slide 53 ![Diagram: Slide 53 Image 1](/uploads/2.2 Epidemiology_s53_img1.jpg) --- ## Slide 54 ![Diagram: Slide 54 Image 1](/uploads/2.2 Epidemiology_s54_img1.jpg) --- ## Slide 55 Implications for Disease Management Strategies Monocyclic Diseases Reduce the amount of primary inoculum, or affect the efficiency of invasion by the primary inoculum. Polycyclic Diseases Reducing the amount of primary inoculum has less impact. Reducing the rate of increase of the pathogen more beneficial. --- ## Slide 56 Principles of epidemiology indicates that control measures can do this in only two ways. 1. They may reduce (or delay) disease at the beginning of the season (x) or 2. They may decrease the rate of disease development (r) during the growing period. --- ## Slide 57 Ways to reduce disease (inoculum) at beginning (x0) Affects monocyclic and polycylic diseases Certified seed Sanitation Seed treatments Quarantine Host plant resistance Ways to decrease the rate of disease development (infection rate) (r) Change the environment Fertilizer application Host plant resistance Ways to change (t) Harvest early before disease becomes severe. Plant early (cereal cyst nematode) --- ## Slide 58 PATTERNS OF EPIDEMICS Interactions among the elements of epidemics, as influenced over time by factors of the environment and by human interference, are expressed in patterns and rates.
